channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: BoardGameGeek Fires Advertising Manager Over Board Game, Possess Me, Satan
item.content:encoded:
BoardGameGeek has fired its advertising manager over a bit of tabletop game drama. That drama involves the manager's handling of ads for Possess Me, Satan, a board game currently being crowdfunded on Gamefound.

The Possess Me, Satan and BoardGameGeek Advertising Debacle

According to a report by Board Game Wire, the situation began when the developers of Possess Me, Satan, Falling Whale Games, reached out to BoardGameGeek's advertising manager Chad Krizan to have ads for their crowdfunding campaign on the website.

Things took a turn when Krizan stated in an e-mail exchange to Falling Whale that he could not "in good conscience" approve ads for the game because "the thought of displaying this subject matter makes me sick to my stomach." As the emails progressed, Krizan stated that he knew people in his life who suffered from demonic possession and that it devastated real lives.

Falling Whale then inquired if this meant Possess Me, Satan ads were being rejected on religious grounds and asked for clarification on the exact grounds of BGG's advertising policy they were violating. Krizan response appeared to be that the project was in bad taste.

“Keep in mind it’s not over religion, but reality. It’s the same reason I would say a game would be in very poor taste if it featured being a sexual predator, or something that would directly trigger someone that’s been harmed by the subject matter.

“It’s about keeping BGG welcoming to everyone, and since I’m privy to this subject matter, I know firsthand that this is not friendly content, and incredibly triggering, put in front of some of the population that visits BGG.”

On paper, this situation seems bad on behalf of the developers of Possess Me, Satan, but not unexpected. Taste and context is a factor when you're trying to promote a product. There's also the danger of a game, like the cancelled Prison Architect board game, potentially perpetuating real-world institutional oppression.  But upon closer inspection, the matter may be overblown. 

Indie games media site Gametrodon got a hold of the ad banners the developers were planning to use. Those ads showed basic artwork, some banners, and a stereotypical version of Satan (red skin, spaded tail, horns, etc.) in a business suit. There is very little in terms of overt supernatural symbols or phrases, not even a cliche pentagram.

As for the actual board game itself, its subject matter is similarly superficial. The campaign is advertised as a social deduction game where players take on different roles in a ghost hunt gone wrong, leading to a demonic possession. Players get different roles like Chaotic, Loner, or Possessed. The roles have different goals. Guess the person possessed by the devil correctly and you win.

The email correspondence between the developers and Krizan went viral on social media, including Reddit, and shortly after BGG announced they had fired Krizan. 

The founder of BGG, Scott Alden, stated in a thread (now locked) on the site, stating that Krizan responded inappropriately to Falling Whale Games and that it, "does not reflect or represent our company or the way we conduct business."

Ever since he was small, Tyler Chancey has had a deep, abiding love for video games and a tendency to think and overanalyze everything he enjoyed. This… More about Tyler



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/news/boardgamegeek-fires-advertising-manager-over-board-game-possess-me-satan
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Beyond Good and Evil 2 and Splinter Cell Missing in Ubisoft's Future Plans
item.content:encoded:
This time of year is always interesting, because many company's are reporting their results for the 'fiscal year' previously - often running March to March. Ubisoft is no exception, and with the company in upheaval and making many changes, like cancelling 6 projects earlier this year, with the only known one being the Prince of Persia: Sands of Time Remake.

With that going on, and all the studio musical chairs, examining their planned outlook and what they are saying to reassure investors can help us understand what the company is hoping to do... and what they aren't counting on so much. Two IP stood out as not being mentioned in their report or conference call, despite having been in the works for a while and not having been announced as cancelled at this time. Beyond Good and Evil 2 has had a troubled development, but if released would presumably be expected to be a major release for Ubisoft as they reinvigorate an IP, and make some money on decades of development work. 

The other title has been in the weeds for less time, but the Splinter Cell Remake was announced in 2021, and the studio faced layoffs earlier this year. This would be another one of their successful Tom Clancy brand lines, and after 5  years of development, one would expect that it is an AAA game, and thus would be notable.

Instead, for the Fiscal Years of 2027-2028, and 2028-2029, Ubisoft mentions a "stronger and diversified content pipeline' supported by major brands like Assassin's Creed, Far Cry, and Ghost Recon, as well as live services for games like Rainbow Six Siege. They do mention that Fiscal Year 2026-2027 will have other premium games based on Ubisoft IP beyond these titles and The Division 2, but a lack of mention of these long MIA games, or even veiled references to the IPs is quite notable.

How is Ubisoft doing financially?

In more boring fiscal news, Ubisoft slightly out performed their expectations for last year, due to a stronger back-catalog but it still performed worse then the year before as they were doing all this little rearranging this year. Ubisoft is expecting this next year to be a rough one with a weaker release schedule. They anticipate Blag Flag Resynched, unannounced games, and live service growth for Rainbow Siege: Siege to stop the company from hemoragging too much money, though it expects to still lose money on the year.

We reached out to Ubisoft for comment regarding Beyond Good and Evil 2, and Splinter Cell Remake, but did not immediately hear back grom them. We will update this story if more becomes available.

A longtime lover of speculative fiction, in almost all its forms, Don joined TechRaptor in 2014 on a whim sending in an application as he was looking for… More about Don



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/beyond-good-and-evil-2-and-splinter-cell-missing-in-ubisofts-future-plans
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
